articles.author: Turtle Net
content: Turtle Net is dedicated to educate and entertain those who stumble across it.
It is designed as a place of quick and easy education on turtles, especially for younger people or those who do not have much of an understanding on turtles.

At the same time, it also serves as a form of entertainment and outlet of creativity by allowing users to explore, upload, and create posts related to turtles. By limiting the user to only uploading things related to turtles, they must truly use their creativity. At the same time, it may also provide educational value to those who view it.
Turtle Net is meant for users to come together under a single topic and a single cause, and that is turtles. It is meant for creativity, learning, and fun.
... It is meant for you.

articles.genre: how to take care of your turtle (easy read)
articles.author: Turtle Net
content: A very common mistake in slider turtle care is the diet. Many owners unintentionally feed only pellets for long periods of time without realizing that turtles require a much more varied diet.

While pellets are an important part of a slider’s diet, feeding pellets exclusively can lead to longterm health issues. However, this is a very common situation and is usually due to misinformation rather than neglect.

Many pet stores recommend pellets as the main food source, but in reality, they should only make up a small portion of the overall diet. All slider turtles are omnivores, and their diet should be mostly leafy greens with smaller amounts of pellets and occasional treats.
For a healthy slider turtle, it helps to follow a consistent weekly feeding routine. The following is an example of a balanced schedule.
[ex.] On Monday, offer leafy greens such as dandelion greens or romaine lettuce. No pellets are needed.
[ex.] On Tuesday, offer another serving of greens, such as collard greens or mustard greens, to provide dietary variety and encourage healthy eating habits.
[ex.] On Wednesday, feed a small portion of pellets. This should be a limited meal rather than the main food source.
[ex.] On Thursday, return to leafy greens. Aquatic plants can also be offered if available.
[ex.] On Friday, offer leafy greens again. Consistency helps establish greens as the primary food source.
[ex.] On Saturday, offer another small pellet feeding.
[ex.] On Sunday, offer leafy greens or, optionally, give a light feeding break. Some keepers choose not to feed adult turtles every day, but this is not required.

Fruit such as strawberries should only be offered as an occasional treat, given once a month or less. While not exactly harmful in small amounts, fruit is high in sugar and low in the nutrients slider turtles need for a balanced diet

If a turtle is coming from a pellet heavy diet, lightly sprinkling crushed pellets over greens can help encourage eating at first. This should be slowly reduced over time so the turtle learns to eat greens on their own.
If your slider turtle has any special conditions, health concerns, or dietary restrictions, please consult a qualified reptile veterinarian for personalized advice for your turtle.

articles.author: Turtle Net
content: Slider turtles are a group of freshwater pond turtles native to the central and southeastern United States and parts of Mexico. They are one of the most commonly recognized pet turtle groups in the world.
Quick fun facts: 1. Slider turtles can live 20–40 years ||| 2. Slider Turtles get their name from being able to slide off rocks and logs quickly after basking.
The three most common species are the Red-eared Slider, Yellow-bellied Slider, and Cumberland Slider. They are very related and share very similar behaviors and habitats.
As adults, they normally reach about 5–12 inches (which is 13–30 cm) in shell length, but it depends on the species and sex.

(Diet under Captivity) Most slider turtles are omnivores, eating both meats and plants. When in captivity, you should feed them commercial turtle pellets as about 1/4 of their diet (as the animal protein portion), along with occasional protein sources such as earthworms, bloodworms, or small insects. Fruits should only be given as an occasional treat (such as a small piece of strawberry every 2–3 weeks), while the rest of their diet should consist mainly of leafy greens and vegetables. The best vegetables depend on the species, but commonly include dandelion greens as the top staple, along with others such as collard greens, romaine lettuce, mustard greens, and carrot tops. Instead of feeding the same foods every day, you should rotate them. For example: 2 days a week you may give pellets along with a small amount of mixed greens (dandelion, collard, romaine, carrot tops), 1 day higher-protein feeding (pellets plus earthworm or bloodworm), 1 day dandelion greens with a small amount of pellets, 1 day collard greens and romaine lettuce mix, 1 day mixed greens rotation, and 1 day light feeding or rest day. Any leftover food should be removed from the tank daily using something like a net to maintain proper water quality and hygiene. Younger slider turtles should receive more meats, while older ones should have a much more balanced diet. Fun fact: slider turtles are attracted to red foods, such as red bell peppers, which should only be given as an occasional treat.

(Natural Diet) Most slider turtles are omnivores, eating both meats and plants. Their diet changes as they age. Younger sliders eat more protein filled foods, like insects and small fish, while adults eat more plants such as aquatic vegetation and algae.

Slider turtles live in freshwater environments such as ponds, lakes, marshes, and slowly moving rivers. They heavily rely on basking areas like rocks and logs in order to regulate their body temperature, or keep their temperature balanced.
They are cold blooded reptiles, meaning they depend on external heat sources like sunlight and can not heat themselves. When under captivity, they require UVB lighting and heat lamps to stay healthy.

Slider turtles are good swimmers and spend much of their time in water, but they often climb out to bask in the sun. Basking helps them dry their shells and absorb heat and UV light. They also need the heat since they are cold blooded.
In many parts of the world, especially outside their native range, slider turtles are considered invasive. They can compete with native species for food and habitat.

One major cause of their spread is pet release. People often release unwanted pet turtles into the wild, even if the animal isn't local to that area, which can harm local ecosystems and is illegal in most places.
As pets, slider turtles require large tanks, strong filtration systems, UVB lighting, and a longterm commitment since they can live for decades under good conditions.
Common misconceptions include that they can survive in small tanks or without UV light, which is false and often leads to health problems or even death in captivity.

Like most freshwater turtles, slider turtles are not exactly fast on land, but when motivated (such as from being panicked or being startled) they have huge bursts of energy, making them incredibly fast on land for just a few seconds.
Sliders have bad hearing but instead are very sensitive to vibrations. This can make it hard to sneak up on them, as they will sense it before you can even get close.
Another fun fact is that slider turtles can recognize feeding routines and often respond to movement near their tank when they associate it with food. They remember who feeds them!

articles.author: Turtle Net
content: Box turtles and semi-aquatic turtles are often confused for one-another. However, they are very different.

Semi-aquatic turtles includes all freshwater turtles, such as pond turtles (which also includes slider turtles and many more). They spend most of their time in water and are excellent swimmers. When kept as a pet, they require a large tank, a water filter, a UVB & heat light, and one or more basking platforms. They often live in freshwater environments like ponds, lakes, marshes, and slowly moving rivers. They come out of the water when they need to bask under the sun (often on a rock or log) since they are cold-blooded reptiles and need UVB light for their shell.

Box turtles can swim, but not as fast as aquatic turtles. They do not go in the water much and can only walk along the bottom of the water body or swim for a short distance. When kept as a pet, they require a large backyard with a large pond and fencing or another barrier so they can not escape. However, sometimes they can climb fences. They normally live in grassy areas, but often are found near freshwater water bodies, like ponds and streams because they need water to regulate their body temperature and to stay hydrated.
So, box turtles are mostly land turtles, while semi-aquatic turtles mostly live in the water. Furthermore, many box turtles are larger than most semi-aquatic turtles, but it depends.
Now, next time you see either of these turtle categories, you'll be able to identify them based off their behavior and location.

articles.author: Turtle Net
content: Sea Turtles are very unique. They are large with very big flippers to help them swim. They are found in lukewarm to hot salt water bodies like oceans. They are cold-blooded and need the environmental temperature (in this case, the water temperature) to be warm to survive, just like every other turtle. There are only 7 sea turtle species on the planet, and 6 of them can be found in the U.S.

There are seven living species of sea turtles: Green, Loggerhead, Leatherback, Hawksbill, Olive Ridley, Kemp's Ridley, and Flatback. Leatherbacks are the largest and can weigh over 450 kg (1,000 pounds). Kemp's Ridleys are the smallest and rarest (by population) sea turtle species. Unlike the others, Leatherbacks do not have a hard shell. Instead, they have a flexible, leathery shell.

As of June 2026, all species of sea turtles are protected under the U.S. Endangered Species Act. The Hawksbill and the Kemp's Ridley are labeled critically endangered on the IUCN Red List. The Leatherback, Loggerhead, and Olive Ridley are labeled vulnerable, with a less high risk. Green Sea Turtles are labeled least concern by the IUCN globally (they changed from Endangered to Least Concern in October, 2025), but some specific, regional populations are labeled differently. Flatback sea turtles do not have enough population data to be given a label.

The diet depends on the species. According to seaturtlespacecost.com, Green sea turtles eat algae, seagrasses, and seaweed. Leatherbacks eat "jellies and other soft-bodied invertebrates like tunicates and sea squirts". Loggerheads eat crabs, conchs, whelks, and horseshoe crabs. Hawksbills eat mostly sea sponges. Olive Ridleys eat crabs, shrimp, lobster, sea urchins, jellies, algae, and fish. Kemp's Ridleys eat crabs, fish, jellies, shrimp, and mollusks. Flatbacks eat sea cucumbers, jellies, corals, shrimp, crabs, mollusks, fish, and seaweed.

Sea turtles generally only leave the water if they're a female needing to lay eggs. They typically lay about 110 eggs per nest, and females lay about 2 to 8 nests every season (March through October). The sex of the sea turtle depends on the temperature of the egg. If the egg is cooler or ~27.7°C (81.8°F), it will be male. If the egg is warmer or above 31°C (87.8°F), it will be female. Due to global warming, it is believed sea turtles are primarily female because female eggs hatch under warmer conditions. Sea turtles remember the beach they were born and often lay their own eggs there when they become old enough.

When baby sea turtles hatch, they usually emerge from the nest together at night or sunset. By instinct, they move toward the brightest visible light, which is normally the moonlight or sunset reflecting off the ocean. Unfortunately, bright lights from the opposite direction will confuse them.

Sea turtles have feeding grounds (also known as a foraging ground). This is the area where they will primarily eat and live in. However, sea turtles migrate very frequently and may travel hundreds or thousands of miles to lay eggs or mate. After laying eggs, female sea turtles return to their feeding grounds.

Sea turtles are long living reptiles. Although it is difficult to determine their exact age in the wild, a lot of scientists believe they can live for 50 years or more. Most species also take decades to reach adulthood before they begin reproducing.

Sea turtles face many threats. Plastic pollution (plastic bags look like jellyfish), fishing nets, boat strikes, coastal development, climate change, and poaching all contribute to declining populations. When they hatch, there are even more temporary dangers: birds, waves pushing them away, and more. Even artificial lights near beaches (like large skyscrapers) can confuse hatchlings, causing them to crawl away from the ocean instead of toward it. Most hatchlings that go away from the ocean sadly die from dehydration, exhaustion, predators, or vehicles, which reduces their chances of survival by a ton.

Fun fact: Sea turtles have special salt glands near their eyes that remove extra salt from the seawater they drink. This can make them look as if they are crying, but they are actually getting rid of extra salt!

Fun fact 2: Sea turtles are great divers. While resting, some species can stay underwater for many hours by slowing their heart rate. During swimming or feeding, they usually surface much more often to breathe.

articles.author: Turtle Net
content: Snapper turtles are large freshwater turtles best known for their very strong jaws and defensive behavior when they feel threatened. They are part of the genus Chelydra (a genus is a group of related living things) and are commonly found in North America.

Snapper turtles live in slowly moving freshwater environments such as ponds, lakes, swamps, marshes, and rivers. They prefer to stay on the bottoms of the water body (which is often muddy) where they can hide and attack prey.

They mostly stay alone and are known for their defensive snapping behavior when out of water. In water, however, they are often calmer and rather prefer to avoid confrontation by other animals by staying hidden.
Snapper turtles are omnivores (eats both meats and plants) but eat more meat than many other freshwater turtles. They eat fish, amphibians, insects, worms, small mammals, carrion, and aquatic plants.
They can grow very big, with common snapping turtles reaching 20–45 inches (8-18 in) in shell length, while alligator snapping turtles can grow even bigger.

Female snapping turtles lay their eggs on land, lots of times traveling long distances from water just to find a good nesting site. A typical clutch (like a nest) may contain dozens of eggs. The peak seasons for snapper turtle egg laying are June and July.
Snapper turtles should not be messed with in the wild. When threatened, they may bite as a form of defense, but they usually prefer to retreat into water rather than attack.
In some areas, snapping turtles are considered invasive because they are not native there, often due to pet release.

articles.author: Turtle Net
content: Tortoises are a type of turtle. This means that all tortoises are turtles, but not all turtles are tortoises. Unlike most other turtles which are either mostly aquatic or fully aquatic, tortoises are strictly land-dwelling, meaning they only go on land and can not swim.

Tortoises do not go in water. Forcing them to swim may cause them to drown. Tortoises are mostly very slow but steady, unlike most freshwater turtles which have unsteady land speeds because they can have bursts of speed on land when motivated but then quickly slow down.

Most tortoises are herbivores, eating mostly grasses and weeds. Vegatables can be an occasional treat, but many tortoise species can not have certain veggies (common examples include Avocado, rhubarb, and spinach, and lettuce), so you should do further research for the specific species. Either way, a tortoise should not have them frequently. Some tortoise species (like Red-footed and Yellow-footed Tortoises) can have specific kinds of fruit (often including fruits like bananas, mangoes, berries, and figs). When kept in captivity, you can also buy prepackaged tortoise pellet food depending on the species, but that should not make up all of its diet.

Different tortoise species have slightly different dietary needs depending on where they live, so the paragraph above should be taken with a grain of salt. For example, Mediterranean tortoises eat mostly grasses and weeds, while some tropical species eat a mix of leafy plants and occasional fruit.
Tortoises require a high-calcium diet to help keep healthy shell growth and bone strength. Many caretakers provide calcium supplements or natural cuttlebone to ensure proper nutrition.
Even though tortoises are land animals, they still require constant access to clean fresh water for drinking and soaking, especially in hot climates or seasons.
Common mistakes include feeding too much fruit, offering iceberg lettuce (which has close to no nutrients), or giving animal protein, which most tortoise species do not need.
